titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Faber Birren Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Sarah Tunney

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 4301862.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday September 3, 2020 at 23:00

did.abstract: The papers document Faber Birren's career as an authority on color, particularly during the years 1968-1989. They focus on his scholarship and his endowment of the Birren Collection.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift to the Art and Architecture Library from Faber Birren and family, 1971-1998; transferred from the Art and Architecture Library to Manuscripts and Archives, 1998 and 2004; transferred from Manuscripts and Archives to the Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library, 2011.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research. Box 12 is closed until January 1, 2018, unless curatorial permission is granted.

Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has not been transferred to Yale University.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Faber Birren Papers, Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library, Yale University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Faber Birren (September 21, 1900 - December 30, 1988), noted authority on color, was born and raised in Chicago, Illinois. His father, Joseph P. Birren, was a successful landscape painter, and his mother, Crescentia (Lang) Birren, a skilled pianist. He attended classes at the Art Institute of Chicago while still in high school, and completed two years (1919-1921) at the University of Chicago studying color theory. Birren published his first articles on color in 1924, and his first book on color four years later. By the end of his life, he had authored a total of 254 articles and forty books on the subject of color. Over a period of fifty-four years, Faber Birren was retained as a color consultant by companies such as Dupont, Walt Disney and General Electric, as well as by the United States Army, Coast Guard and Navy. In 1971, he established a depository and an endowment at Yale University for books on color. The Faber Birren Collection) has grown from his initial donation of 266 books into the most comprehensive collection of its kind in the world, and numbers over 2000 items. Birren was married for fifty-four years to Wanda (Martin) Birren. They had two daughters, Zoe and Fay, and four grandchildren.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Faber Birren, noted authority on color, was born September 21, 1900, in Chicago, Illinois. The son of successful landscape painter, Joseph P. Birren, and skilled pianist, Crescentia (Lang) Birren, Faber was the youngest of three children (Jeanette Birren Quinn (b. 1894) and William B. Birren (b. 1898). After attending Mount Carmel Roman Catholic Elementary School, Birren studied art and ceramics at Nichols Senn High School. During his last two years there, Faber also attended classes at the Art Institute of Chicago. After high school, he entered the University of Chicago (1919). It was then that he began his study of color theory under Walter Sargent and others. Birren left the University of Chicago after only two years, and in 1921, he began working for a local bookseller and publisher, Charles T. Powner. It was in this first job that Birren began to collect books on color and to write on the subject.

In 1924, Birren published his first articles (3) on color; the same year, his earliest attempt at a book on color was unsuccessfully submitted for publication. It was not until 1928 that Color in Vision was published. For the next several years and through the Great Depression, Birren published scores of articles and stories on the functional use of color, especially its use in hospitals and schools. Meanwhile, he continued to submit manuscripts on color and novels to publishers. Birren later explained that the meager pay for a published article coupled with the economic state of the country in 1933, "led to the endeavor to become a free-lance professional color authority." Birren reoriented his focus from writing to consulting.

Until late in life, Faber Birren was retained as a color consultant by companies such as Dupont, Walt Disney, General Electric, Westvaco Corp., and Sears & Roebuck, as well as by the United States Navy, Coast Guard and Army. During World War II, he specialized in the functional applications of color, focusing on its use in factories and war industries. He also developed color safety standards which were internationally adopted.

After the war, Birren continued his color consulting, and began lecturing at museums, universities and other institutions. He was a prolific author. By the end of his life, Birren had authored and published 254 articles and forty books on color; he had also published three novels under the pseudonyms Gregor and Martin Lang.

In 1971, Birren offered part of his personal collection of books on color to Yale University. Along with his donation to the university of over two hundred rare books, he also established an endowment and a research and publication fund for the development of a collection of books on color. The Birren Collection is the most comprehensive collection of books on color in the world.

In the same spirit of patronage, Birren endowed the Faber Birren Color Award Show in 1981, through the Stamford Connecticut Art Association. Birren created this competition in the hope of sparking interest in the field of color. The annual award is given for "distinguished and creative expression with color... in any medium."

In 1934, Birren married Wanda Martin (May 10, 1908 - Dec. 31, 1997), who was supportive of her husband's endeavors. They had two daughters, Zoe (b. Sept. 22, 1935) and Fay (b. May 1, 1939), and four grandchildren. Faber Birren died at age eighty-eight on December 30, 1988, after suffering a stroke.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Faber Birren Papers document the endowment and development of the Birren Collection at Yale University. Additionally, there is documentation on the research projects in which Birren engaged during the last twenty years of his life. There is little written documentation on his career as an industrial color expert.

The Faber Birren Papers are one of several resources to be found at Yale University on Faber Birren. Researchers should also consult the Arts Library Special Collections. A significant portion of the Birren Collection is available on microform, which can be purchased through Primary Source Media, Hamden, Connecticut, via URL: http://www.psmedia.com.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four series and two additions: I. Personal, 1956-1992. II. Correspondence, 1963-1998. III. Writings, 1946-1988. IV. Visual Materials, 1942-1997.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Personal Series is arranged alphabetically, and is most useful in obtaining biographic and bibliographic information on Birren and on the Birren Collection. It also includes lists of published articles and books by Birren.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Correspondence Series contains several hundred letters, arranged alphabetically by subject of correspondence. The majority of the series documents the establishment of the Birren Collection at Yale University. There is correspondence between Birren and staff at the Yale University Art & Architecture Library about additions to the collection and research purposes to which it might be put.

The remainder of the series documents his research (often intense and dogged) on subjects about which he wanted to write books. There is, for example, his decade-long correspondence with museum curators and librarians concerning his project, "The Artist and His Palette." As with several other projects, it is unclear whether this particular project culminated in publication.

did.unittitle: Coloritto , by J. C. Le Blon
Faber Birren Collection of Books on Color

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

did.unittitle: Natural Systems of Colour [by Moses Harris]
Treatise on Painting [by Leonardo da Vinci]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Writings Series is arranged in two sections: articles and books. The articles are published and are arranged alphabetically by title. They are the primary source of information in the collection on Birren's theories on colors and their functional use, particularly as they evolved in his early career. The books section is arranged alphabetically by title, the same order in which Birren maintained the files. It is comprised of notes by the author, manuscripts, correspondence regarding reproduction rights, and photographs. As noted previously, the publication status of several of the projects is uncertain. The manuscripts, therefore, may be quite valuable in documenting his scholarship. The majority of the book section consists of the artists' files, including primarily black and white photographs of artists' works, and a typed manuscript for the unpublished work, "Color Harmony."

did.unittitle: Artists' files [black and white photographs, articles]
Treatise on Painting [by Leonardo da Vinci]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Visual Materials Series is arranged alphabetically by medium. It primarily contains pamphlets, photographs and posters. Photographs comprise the bulk of the series, the majority of which are black and white images. Photographs of Birren's color studies and other artwork, an exhibition, and his Photo-archive of Color Palettes , are all in black and white, rather than in color. The color photographs include exteriors of industrial buildings and factories, and images of machinery, to which Birren applied his "color programs," and snapshots from lecture tours and a birthday party.

did.unittitle: Art [works of art by Birren; black and white photographs]
Pastels à la gerbe [book by Roché; color photographs of miscellaneous pages; slides]
Photo-archive of Color Palettes [includes black and white photographs of artists' palettes, and collected materials]
Stamford Art Association [Salute to Faber Birren...]
Correspondence relating to Faber Birren Collection of Books on Color

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 12 is closed until January 1, 2018, unless curatorial permission is granted.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence is arranged alphabetically by subject of the correspondence. Folders may contain correspondence with several people on the same topic, as well as related printed material by the correspondent or others.

did.unittitle: Treatise on Painting , by Leonardo daVinci, proposed new edition by Birren
Aid for Retarded facility, Stamford, Connecticut
Publications and articles re: experiments in art and technology; effects of color on health and human behavior, etc.
Treatise on Painting , by Leonardo da Vinci, new edition by Birren
Scrapbook of printed material by and about Birren

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains a videocassette that was transferred to the Faber Birren Papers on 2014-12-04.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This item is also individually cataloged in Orbis.
